# The Quiet Death of the Personal Blog

I started blogging in 2004. Back then, everyone I knew in tech had a blog.
We wrote about our projects, our opinions, the books we were reading. Nobody
was optimizing for search engines because RSS was the distribution channel
and Google Reader was the interface.

That world is gone. The open web is being consolidated into a handful of
platforms, and the personal blog is an endangered species.

I've been tracking this decline for a decade. According to an analysis by
WebFinger Analytics, the number of active personal blogs with at least one
post per month dropped from roughly 12 million in 2015 to just over 3
million in 2025. That's a 75 % decline in ten years.

> "The independent web is becoming a museum. Beautiful, curated, and empty."
>
> — Tom Harrington, developer and blogger, in a 2025 newsletter

I don't think the platforms are evil. Substack, Medium, and even LinkedIn's
article feature give writers distribution they could never achieve on a
self-hosted WordPress site. But there's a cost. When your content lives on
someone else's domain, you're renting your relationship with your audience.

Here's what I think we lose when personal blogs disappear:

- **Experimentation.** A blog lets you try a weird format or an unpopular
  opinion without A/B testing it against a corporate style guide.
- **Archival integrity.** Platform-hosted content disappears when the
  platform pivots or dies. My 2004 posts are still at the same URLs.
- **Personality.** There is no algorithm that rewards genuine idiosyncrasy.

I don't have a solution. I'm not sure there is one. But I'll keep this
server running, and I hope a few thousand other stubborn people will too.
